humanities_role_explanation: The benchmark uses humanities material—specifically history, geography, and language/linguistics questions from school examinations—as testing content to gauge general intelligence and cultural alignment, rather than engaging with humanities as an active, domain-specific methodological inquiry.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Multimodal multiple-choice questions where text is paired with images of historical figures, diagrams, maps, or scripts.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Tasks require parsing linguistic features (e.g., matching Swahili idioms or transliterating Javanese script), reading comprehension of literary/historical texts, factual historical QA (such as identifying Beijing's suburban reforms or Mahatma Gandhi's activities), and reasoning over visual representations (such as historical photos, maps, and diagrams).

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Evaluates LLMs across broad domains, including science, mathematics, language, and social sciences.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates closed-source generative LLMs (GPT-4, ChatGPT, Claude) and open-source models (BLOOM, Vicuna, BLIP-2, InstructBLIP, Fromage, OpenFlamingo).

llm_is_primary_system.note: The LLMs are the primary systems being benchmarked.

arts_humanities_data.note: Includes substantial materials relevant to History, Language and Linguistics, and Literature and Literary Theory.

quantitative_metrics.note: Uses accuracy as the quantitative evaluation metric.

english_language.note: The paper is written in English, and English is one of the benchmarked languages.

summary: M3Exam is a multilingual, multimodal, and multilevel benchmark derived from real-world human school exams across nine countries. It evaluates LLM capabilities on diverse linguistic scripts, visual exam questions, and varying educational difficulties (low, mid, high school levels).
key_findings: GPT-4 outperforms other models with 72.92% average accuracy but struggles with low-resource (Javanese) and non-Latin script (Thai) languages. Multimodal models perform poorly on complex visual questions (BLIP-2 scores under 50% overall). Notably, LLMs do not exhibit a monotonic decrease in performance as exam levels increase, contrasting sharply with human cognitive development.
